speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape three of the interview with Marion Horn. I'm [? Riska ?] [? Waslath ?]. Today is September 13, 1995. You were telling me about you had just arrived in Sarajevo.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Sarajevo was definitely a camp, uh, more prepared for the amassing of more people. And there was no open spaces where you could roam, uh, like in the camp before. And we were not told anything. And we were just arrived there. You know, that was, that was it. Um, for no apparent reason about five days later, we were told that we would be leaving that camp and go further down to Bosnia, closer to down south to the Dalmatian coast area, but not-- we were not told where. We were just told we were going further south. And we were put again on trains. Um, and we were, we arrived in a city, Muslim city. Now, Sarajevo is a basically Muslim city. And so is Mostar. And there, we arrived. Uh, I remember it was late afternoon. We were given stars to wear at that point, um, yellow star in the front and in the back. And we were walked from the railroad through the town to whatever destination. We didn't know. And that's when I had the first experience of having stones thrown at me, and predominantly by kids shouting, you know dirty Jews, rotten Jews, uh, whatever. Um, they were not very large stones. But they were stones, nevertheless. And we were taken to a schoolhouse. It was an empty schoolhouse. And I remember there were closets in the classrooms, you know, like armoire-type of closets where teachers kept their supplies. And I remember some of the men taking these closets and turning them down and putting them on the ground, and then taking the shelves out to make a bed. In other words, it was like almost an enclosure, to feel like, uh, some people were fighting over it. Uh, we weren't fighting over it. So I think some of the younger children. My father, being King Solomon, always said, well, look, why don't the baby, the young children-- put the young children in there so that they have a place to feel secure and snuggle.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your father was in a leadership capacity in these camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was the elder. He was considered like the elder in the group and respected. And, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is still the group of 286 that started?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, by that time--

speaker: interviewer
text: Now it was smaller.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was like maybe 260 or whatever. Uh, the money that I had hidden, that went, by the way, to the kids who did the patrol for the rats, and who speared the rats. And that went to [SIGH] some of the people in Mostar, in town, to try and get some milk for the children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why would you, why would your father pay the children who speared the rats? [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: To give them the incentive, and so they should have something in their pocket. That was my father. Um, try to buy from the farmers who would come in to get some food for the children, at least for the young people and for the older people, whatever was available, whether it was milk, or maybe-- an egg was an unheard of thing, but just something.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many small children were there in this group?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was the youngest of the girls. There were a couple of boys that were younger than I. I would say, roughly speaking, there were about 30 children in the group. My brother was among the older ones. He was six years older than I. And so we were in Mostar. Again, it was a school, Germans at that point, but the Wehrmacht, which was the, the army, um, not violent. Um, they were doing a job. They didn't like it. They would have, you know, but they had a job to do. And nothing terribly traumatic happened for a few weeks. And then one night, two very drunk Ustasha came, each one with two pistols, one in each hand. And he said, we want to round out the girls. All the girls are going out on the bus. It was around one, two o'clock in the morning-- um, a lot of very hysterical mothers, very frightened children. We put together some belongings, and we got on the bus.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were among them.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was the youngest in this group. And we didn't know where we were going. The guys were drunk. I didn't even think they-- nobody could think that they could even drive a bus. And they drove into the mountains. And that's very mountainous, uh, area around there. They drove for hours. Early morning, we got to a village, opened up the bus, and said OK. Um, the underground, or the partisans, were fighting in that area. They were called, at that point, the Cetnici.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: C-N-T-C-I- chi chi C-C-I. Cetnici. They were the underground. And they were burning villages, destroying villages in the area. And they wanted us girls to clean up the rubble. And I remember, the climate was very hot, very dry, very hot. Um, there was a dead horse. And they told us that we could help ourselves to the meat if we wanted to and cook it. And some people did.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many were in your group?

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was the oldest?

speaker: interviewee
text: About 19. Um, I found bushes of pomegranate growing wild all over that particular area of the country. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: There were no men with you in this group?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just these two guys.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which two guys?

speaker: interviewee
text: These two Ustasha. Um, they didn't harm any of the girls. Uh, nobody was raped. Uh, we worked very, very hard. It was hard, hard labor, carrying blocks of stones, cleaning roads. Of course, they couldn't get through with the, um, with the trucks through those villages. And [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] it, uh, it was a very frightening, very, very frightening, period. Then they moved us from this particular town and moved us to another town, a village, villages.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the names of these villages?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. There were no-- villages. And unbeknownst to us, from our group in Mostar, they took about 10 of the boys from our same group. And we found them in a couple villages several weeks later. And among the group was my brother and a young man that I later on found in the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you away from the camp in, um, Mostar?

speaker: interviewee
text: How l-- from the school? From that school [INAUDIBLE]? I would say probably about five weeks. Um, my father aged 20 years in that period. Because the most important thing in his life were his children. And we were both taken away. Uh, they took us away again, the girls. We came back to Mostar. And then weeks later, my brother came back with the group. And nobody was seriously hurt, um, just very traumatic, very, very traumatic. Um, then we were taken to another school. I remember a woman in this school who must been the wife or the-- whoever the caretaker of the school was. She had a newborn infant. And she was nursing this infant. And while she was nursing, she used to have a cigarette hanging from the corner of her mouth. And I remember saying to her that it's bad for your health. [LAUGHS] Your child is getting pure nicotine in the mothers milk. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this still in Mostar?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was still in Mostar. Uh, the things that stick in your mind, amazing. She was all yellow, and so was the baby. Um, then we were sent to-- we were packed up one day and said, OK fine. Uh, you're going to somewhere else. And they sent us to a little town called Capljina. C-A-P-L-I-N-A. And we were there for quite awhile. Pomegranate was the main staple. Food was just what was growing, uh, in the area, uh, wheat on the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Stalk.

speaker: interviewee
text: --stalk. Um, rhubarb from the ground, um, really no, no fatty foods. That's for sure. Um, a lot of fighting going on in the mountains all around us. Um, again, it was a abandoned-- more or less, an abandoned, old farm where we were at. And one night, uh, around 2:00 in the morning, the German who was the chief [? major-- ?] there announced that we should all appear in the, in the yard. And when we got outside, they told us to line up. And they had about 10 machine guns pointed at us. And he apologized that they will have to kill us. We have instructions. The Italians are taking over the territory. We definitely are army. We cannot take you with us, and we cannot let you free. And we have instructions that we have to kill you all. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Uh, if you were making a Hollywood movie, it couldn't have been any more effective. Uh, we were going to die. And he said, we are-- the Italians are taking over. We cannot leave them. And suddenly, we heard motors roaring. And I said to the-- somebody next to me, there are motors coming. And they stopped. And you had the distinct feeling that they didn't want to do this, but they had to. They were following orders. And they were really positioning the machine guns. They were going to just-- you know, raze us down. And they, they had promised that it was going to be quick.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] The motors kept coming closer and closer. And it was heavy tanks and trucks. And it was the Italian troops. They were taking over. And the first Italians coming into the area said to the Germans, well, what is, what is going on here?

speaker: interviewee
text: And they said, well, we're leaving. And, uh, we have orders. We can't leave these people. They said, well, just don't worry about it. You just leave. We will, we'll worry about it. Uh, we'll, we'll, we'll take care of them. So the Germans packed up on their trucks and left. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And there was one truck that was marked cucina. That was the army cook. I remember going over to the truck. And there was a little Italian soldier. I said to him, [NON-ENGLISH]. We are so hungry. This is, I think only eight, 10-year-old, nine-year-old child. And he looks at me, mushed up my hair. And he said, little one, I'm going to make you spaghetti. And he took a laundry [? bin. ?] And he boiled spaghetti for everybody. It was the most unbelievable thing when you have that experience and then the contrast. I don't know if I'm expressing-- uh, it was just unbelievable. They were so kind. They were so apologetic. They were so [SIGHS] sorry about what had happened. They said, we don't have the authority to let you people free. But we're not going to kill you. Uh, I feel to this day that the Italians have not gotten enough credit, because there were a few incidents. And I know as a fact that even when Mussolini was ordered to turn the Jews over to Hitler, he didn't do it. It was only until Hitler came into Italy and took over. Uh, we remained there. I can't tell you how long. Uh, the experience was overwhelming.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever learn the name of any of the Italian soldiers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh, I knew the name of this particular man. And my father corresponded with him after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Commander of the unit.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. And, uh, he died a number of years ago. But my father kept in touch with him. Um, they were just-- they said, look, you know, we're here. We don't know why we're here, either. And we would much rather be home with our families and our children. We don't know what we're doing here. And there was an internal war between the Germans and the Italians. They didn't like each other at all. Uh, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] we were there for awhile and then eventually escaped from there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that take place?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother first escaped from the camp, got some help from the Italians, traveled north to Zagreb.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she ever use any of those jewels you once told me about?

speaker: interviewee
text: Made arrangements to get us papers. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Um, she borrowed a doll that I had. I had a Raggedy Ann doll.

speaker: interviewer
text: It traveled all this way with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: It traveled all the way. And I used to just flick it by its arm, because I was not a doll-playing girl. I preferred my pussy cats and my puppies and my [? tangible. ?] But this doll was with me from. I think, the time that I was born. And it was, it was always with me. And when I packed in Samobor among important things, this doll came with me. And my, my mother somehow borrowed the doll. And I didn't pay that much attention to it, but I noticed it when I got it back. The doll had a scar. And it was sutured with very fine stitching.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I said, what happened? My mother said, it ripped. And I fixed it. I had some thread hidden away and a needle. And I fixed it for you. That was, you know, very acceptable. And it wasn't until much, much later that I found out that she had hidden all of her jewelry in there-- not the jewelry, but stones. And eventually, that's what saved our lives. When did she take that out?

speaker: interviewee
text: She took it out little by little. She took it out at this incident. And, well, that's almost like a separate story, our leaving [INAUDIBLE]. Um, we left on our bellies, crawled underneath trains, and hid in the dark in the crevice of tracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then when the train came, we got into the train. It was so quick. I was given orders, and you just-- there was no time to think, how did I do it? Where did we get on the train? All I know is we got on the train. And we traveled north to Zagreb. And just before we arrived in Zagreb-- and Zagreb railroad station had pretty terrible memory to begin with, because that's where we started-- a Gestapo agent got on the train. And he came over. And he said, uh, can I see your papers?

speaker: interviewee
text: We showed him papers. And the papers where, you know, you had religion in, in those, he said, you're Jews. And we said, you have our papers, you know. Uh, he said, I know you're Jews. He said, when we arrive in Zagreb, you are going to follow me to the German comandante, commandat office.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we got off the train. And it was my brother and I walking ahead, and my mother and father following. And we were walking along the station in Zagreb. And we stopped.

speaker: interviewee
text: Um, as we were walking, I nudged my brother. We had a uncanny understanding of each other. And I said to my brother, uh, there's a Italian commandant's office preceding. It was ahead of the one that was further down on a walk down the station. And somehow, my brother and I, we just caught onto each other. We took each other's elbows. We walked into the Italian office, and my parents followed.
